teaching.title: Comparison Operators in C
teaching.analogy: Comparison operators are truth detectors — they take two values, compare them, and report 1 (true) or 0 (false). In C, there's no special boolean type by default — true IS 1 and false IS 0.
teaching.content: C uses the same 6 comparison operators: ==, !=, >, <, >=, <=. They return 1 (true) or 0 (false) as integers. C has NO native boolean — any non-zero int is 'true', 0 is 'false'.

examples.title: C Comparisons Return 0 or 1
examples.explanation: No boolean type — results are integers!

examples.title: C99 bool (optional)
examples.explanation: C99 added <stdbool.h> for proper bool type.

teaching.funFact: In C, if (x = 5) is VALID but dangerous — it ASSIGNS 5 to x (always true since 5 != 0). The classic bug! Many C programmers write if (5 == x) so accidental = causes a compile error. 🐛

teaching.title: Logical Operators — &&, ||, !
teaching.analogy: && demands BOTH sides pass. || lets EITHER side pass. ! flips the result. Same logic as a security system — AND means ALL sensors must trigger, OR means ANY sensor triggers the alarm, NOT inverts the signal.
teaching.content: C uses &&, ||, and ! for boolean operations. They work with integers: 0 is false, non-zero is true. && and || short-circuit — they stop evaluating as soon as the result is known.

examples.title: Logical Operators in C
examples.code:
int age = 20, hasID = 1;

printf("%d\n", age >= 18 && hasID);  // 1 (true)
printf("%d\n", age < 12 || age > 65); // 0 (false)
printf("%d\n", !hasID);              // 0 (NOT true = false)
printf("%d\n", !(age < 18));         // 1 (NOT false = true)

examples.title: Short-Circuit Safety
examples.explanation: Right side skipped when result is already determined.

teaching.funFact: C's short-circuit evaluation is formally documented in the C standard. It's not optional — compilers MUST implement it. This makes null pointer checks like 'if (ptr != NULL && ptr->val > 0)' safe! 🔒

teaching.title: C Arithmetic & Compound Assignment
teaching.analogy: C arithmetic operators are the same as Java's but with one key quirk: integer division TRUNCATES — 7/2 = 3, not 3.5. And C's ++ and -- are everywhere — becoming a core feature of the language's identity!
teaching.content: C arithmetic: +, -, *, /, % (modulo). Integer division truncates. Compound: +=, -=, *=, /=, %=. Pre/post increment: ++x (add before use), x++ (add after use). These are ALL used heavily in C loops.

examples.title: C Arithmetic
examples.code:
printf("%d
", 7 / 2);    // 3  (truncates, not 3.5)
printf("%f
", 7.0 / 2);  // 3.500000
printf("%d
", 7 % 2);    // 1  (remainder)
printf("%d
", -7 % 2);   // -1 (sign follows dividend in C)

examples.title: Pre vs Post Increment
examples.explanation: ++x vs x++ — order matters in expressions.

teaching.funFact: The ++ operator is so iconic it was the inspiration for 'C++' — Bjarne Stroustrup named it C++ as in 'C incremented by one'. A programming pun that stuck! 😄

mcq.q: In C, what does a comparison like (5 > 3) return?
mcq.explanation: Integer Result: C comparisons return 1 (true) or 0 (false) as int values. C has no native boolean — use <stdbool.h> from C99 for bool type, or just use int.

mcq.q: What does 9 % 4 evaluate to in C?
mcq.explanation: Modulo: 9 ÷ 4 = 2 remainder 1. So 9 % 4 = 1. Modulo gives the remainder of integer division.

mcq.q: int x = 5; printf("%d", x++); What prints?
mcq.explanation: Post-Increment: x++ uses the current value (5) FIRST, then increments. printf sees 5, prints 5. After the statement, x becomes 6.
C AND: && is the logical AND in C. Single & is bitwise AND (operates on individual bits). Never mix them up for boolean logic!

mcq.q: int flag = 5; if (flag) { ... }  Does the if block execute?
options: No — flag must be 1 for true
Yes — any non-zero value is true in C
mcq.explanation: C Truthiness: In C, 0 = false, ANY non-zero value = true. So if (flag) with flag=5 is equivalent to if (true). This is different from Java which requires a boolean expression.

coding.title: C Boolean True
coding.objective: In C without stdbool.h, what int value represents true?
coding.starterCode: // In C, true is represented as integer: ___
coding.hint: Any non-zero is true, but 1 is the canonical true value.

coding.title: Pre-increment
coding.hint: ++x increments FIRST then uses value. So 4 is printed.

options: = is assignment not comparison. x=0 assigns 0 to x (falsy), block never runs. Should be: if (x == 0)
debugging.answer: = is assignment not comparison. x=0 assigns 0 to x (falsy), block never runs. Should be: if (x == 0)
debugging.hint: = assigns (changes x to 0, which is false — block never runs). == compares. This is a famous C bug!

options: Single & is BITWISE AND — evaluates both sides. Use && (logical AND) which short-circuits to avoid a/b when b=0
debugging.answer: Single & is BITWISE AND — evaluates both sides. Use && (logical AND) which short-circuits to avoid a/b when b=0
debugging.hint: & evaluates BOTH sides always, even if left is false. && short-circuits — skips right side if left is false. Use && for safety.

prediction.explanation: a>b = true = 1. a-b = 2 > 0 is true (1), b < 5 is true (1). 1 && 1 = 1. Output: 1↵1.

teaching.title: if / else if / else in C
teaching.analogy: C's if statement is the original — Java, C++, and JavaScript all copied their if syntax FROM C. The rules: condition in parentheses, code blocks in curly braces. Simple, powerful, and unchanged since 1972.
teaching.content: C if/else: condition in (), block in {}. C allows any expression as condition — 0 is false, non-zero is true. Unlike Java's strict boolean requirement, C accepts integers directly as conditions.

examples.title: C if / else if / else
C accepts int as condition
examples.explanation: 0 = false, non-zero = true — no bool required.

teaching.funFact: The C if statement was directly adopted by Java (1995), C# (2000), and JavaScript (1995). PHP, Swift, Go — virtually every modern language copied C's if syntax. Dennis Ritchie's design has stood for 50+ years! 🏛️

teaching.title: Dangling else Problem
teaching.analogy: The 'dangling else' is one of C's classic ambiguity traps. If you skip curly braces with nested ifs, which 'if' does the 'else' belong to? The answer: the NEAREST if. This follows a rule but causes many bugs when you assume otherwise.
teaching.content: Without {}, an else binds to the NEAREST preceding if. This is called 'dangling else'. Adding {} makes the code intention explicit and safe. This problem is why style guides mandate always using {} in C.

examples.title: Dangling else — tricky!
examples.explanation: Indentation LIES. The else goes with the inner if!

teaching.funFact: The dangling else problem exists in C, Java, C++, and JavaScript. It's listed in ISO/IEC 9899 (C standard). The solution is always the same: USE CURLY BRACES! 🔒

mcq.q: In C, can you use an integer directly in an if condition?
options: Yes — 0 is false, non-zero is true
mcq.explanation: C Truthy: C's if accepts any expression. 0 = false, anything else = true. if (5) is valid and always true. This is more flexible than Java which requires boolean.

mcq.q: Without {}, a dangling else binds to:
options: The first if in the file
mcq.explanation: Nearest If: By C specification, an else binds to the nearest preceding if without an else. Indentation can be misleading. Always use {} to make your intent clear!

mcq.q: C has how many 'elif' keyword equivalents?
mcq.explanation: C else if: Like Java, C uses 'else if' (two separate words). Python's 'elif' is unique to Python. Both Java and C copied the else if syntax from Algol/C origins.

mcq.q: int n = -5; if (n) { printf("A"); } What prints?
mcq.explanation: Non-Zero = True: -5 is non-zero, so it's true in C. The if block runs → A prints. Only 0 is false in C — negative numbers are true!

coding.title: Integer as Condition
coding.objective: int flag = 1; Does if (flag) { ... } execute? yes or no
coding.hint: 1 is non-zero → true in C. The block runs.

coding.title: Grade Output
coding.objective: int score = 65. if (score>=70) 'C'; else if (score>=60) 'D'; else 'F'. Output?
coding.hint: 65 is not >= 70, but IS >= 60. Second condition matches → D.

options: Without {}, only the first printf is inside the if. 'Congrats' always prints. Add {} to include both.
debugging.answer: Without {}, only the first printf is inside the if. 'Congrats' always prints. Add {} to include both.
debugging.hint: Without curly braces, only the immediately next statement is inside the if. Use {} when you want multiple statements in the block.

prediction.explanation: x=0 is false → A skipped. x+1 = 1 which is non-zero = true → B prints.

teaching.title: switch in C
teaching.analogy: C's switch is the original multi-way branch — Java, C++, and JavaScript all derived their switch from C. Pick a 'channel' (case), tune in, and it plays. Without break, it 'falls through' to the next channel automatically — a feature sometimes used intentionally.
teaching.content: C switch works with int and char types (NOT strings — C has no String type). Cases must be integer constants or char literals. Without break, execution falls through. default handles unmatched values.

examples.title: C switch with int
examples.explanation: C switch: int or char only. No strings!

examples.title: switch with char
examples.explanation: char works too — common in C for menus.

teaching.funFact: C switch canNOT use strings (char arrays). This is because switch uses integer values internally. To branch on strings in C, you must use if/else with strcmp(). Java relaxed this rule in Java 7! 🔤

teaching.title: Ternary Operator in C
teaching.analogy: C invented the ternary operator ? : — it was there before Java, JavaScript, PHP, and C# all copied it. One line, three operands: condition ? value-if-true : value-if-false. A classic C power tool.
teaching.content: C ternary: condition ? expr1 : expr2. Returns expr1 if condition is non-zero (true), else expr2. Common in C for compact assignment. Works with any data type.

examples.title: Ternary in C

teaching.funFact: The C ternary operator is so efficient that C compilers often compile it to a single conditional move (CMOV) CPU instruction — faster than a branch prediction miss on a full if/else! ⚡

mcq.q: C switch statement works with which types?
mcq.explanation: C Switch Types: C switch only works with integer types (int, char, short, long). Strings (char arrays) are NOT supported — use if/else with strcmp() for string comparison.

mcq.q: What happens in a C switch with no 'break'?
mcq.explanation: C Fall-Through: Without break, C switch falls through — executes the matched case AND all subsequent cases until a break. Sometimes intentional (to group cases), but often a bug.

mcq.q: int x=3; y = (x>5) ? 10 : 20; What is y?
mcq.explanation: Ternary: x=3. 3>5 is false (0) → y gets the FALSE value: 20.

mcq.q: Can you use a string like "Monday" as a switch case value in C?
options: No — C switch only supports int and char
mcq.explanation: No String Switch: C has no native String type. C switch works with integer values. To compare strings, use strcmp() from <string.h> inside if/else chains.

coding.title: Switch Stop
coding.objective: What keyword stops fall-through in a C switch case?
coding.hint: Without it, cases 'fall through' into each other.

options: C switch can't use strings — use if/else with strcmp(day, "Monday") == 0
debugging.answer: C switch can't use strings — use if/else with strcmp(day, "Monday") == 0
debugging.hint: C switch only works with int and char. For strings, use strcmp() from <string.h>.

prediction.explanation: x=2 matches case 2: prints B. No break → falls through to case 3: prints C. break → stops. Output: B↵C.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE C COMPILER
teaching.analogy: The C Compiler is utterly unforgiving. It checks every comparison operator, every break, every dangling else. One wrong = instead of == and your program silently misbehaves. Prove your C logic is bulletproof!
teaching.content: THE C COMPILER tests ALL Level 2 C: integer comparisons returning 0/1, && and ||, short-circuit, if/else if/else, dangling else danger, switch with int/char (no strings), fall-through, ternary operator.

examples.title: Full Level 2 C arsenal
examples.code:
✅ Comparisons return 0/1 (int)
✅ Any non-zero = true in C
✅ &&, ||, ! operators
✅ if / else if / else with ()
✅ Dangling else — use {} always!
✅ switch with int/char only
✅ break to prevent fall-through
✅ Ternary ? : operator

teaching.funFact: The C compiler's strictness is what makes C so fast and so dangerous. No automatic bounds checking, no null safety, no reference counting. The programmer is fully responsible. With great power comes great responsibility! ⚔️

mcq.q: int x = 5; if (x = 0) printf("A"); else printf("B"); What prints?
mcq.explanation: Classic Bug: x = 0 ASSIGNS 0 to x. 0 is false → else runs → B prints. And x is now 0! Using = instead of == accidentally is a very common C bug.

mcq.q: C switch works with char. switch (grade) { case 'A': ... } — is this valid?
options: Yes — char is an integer type in C
mcq.explanation: Char in Switch: In C, char is an integer type (1 byte). switch can use char values, and cases use single-quoted char literals like case 'A':.

mcq.q: int a=1, b=0; printf("%d", a && b || !b); Output?
int x=10; char *r = (x>5) ? "big" : "small"; printf("%s", r); Output?
mcq.explanation: Ternary: x=10, 10>5 is true → returns "big". printf with %s prints the string. Output: big.

mcq.q: Why can't C switch use string (char array) values?
options: Switch is too slow for strings
Switch uses integer comparison internally — arrays can't be compared with ==
mcq.explanation: Integer Switch: C switch compares using integer equality internally. String arrays can't be compared with == (it compares addresses, not content). Use if/else + strcmp() for strings.

coding.title: Boss: Fix the comparison
coding.objective: Change the assignment bug to a comparison in this if condition.
coding.hint: == compares equality. = assigns. Never use = in an if condition unless intentional.

coding.title: Boss: Ternary Max
coding.objective: int a=8, b=3. Use ternary to find the max. (a>b)?a:b = ?
coding.hint: a=8 > b=3 is true → a (8) is max.

options: switch doesn't work with ranges — 85 must exactly match case 85. But score >= 90 etc. needs if/else for range checking
debugging.hint: 85 exactly matches case 85 → prints B → break → done. This is valid C switch! The switch works correctly here.

prediction.explanation: 4%2==0 is true → ternary returns 1. x>10 is false, x<5 is true → false||true = 1. Output: 1↵1.
